chunks.title: Introduction to ApoE4
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the topic of ApoE4, addressing concerns about this genetic polymorphism and its implications for those on an animal-based diet. They mention prior discussions on ApoE4 and set the stage for a deeper dive into its historical context and health impact.
chunks.transcript: okay the next question is from rebecca she says paul i've been hearing a lot about apo e4 or apoe4 i say it apoe4 should i be worried about this how do i know if i have this genetic polymorphism and is it something to be aware of if you are eating an animal-based diet so in the past i've done apoe4 specific podcasts this will just be a little snippet of those and i will start high level and then get a little more specific before moving on

chunks.title: Historical Context of ApoE Variants
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the evolutionary timeline of the ApoE gene variants, primarily focusing on how ApoE4 has been the ancestral form. They highlight its historical prevalence among early humans and its potential protective roles against infections, including its association with a higher risk of dementia in modern populations.
chunks.transcript: but my high level perspective on apo e4 is that if you are insulin sensitive this is probably not something to worry about based on the literature it's important to know that apoe4 is the ancestral genotype of apoe apoe is an apollo of a protein present in our bodies and apoe4 is what all of our primate ancestors had and what all of humans and pre-hominids had for millions of years apoe3 appears to have arisen i believe 200 000 years ago april e2 about 80 000 years ago those numbers could be a little bit give or take but remember that for the majority of pre-hominid and at least half of homo sapiens evolution on this planet everyone on the planet was apoe4 apoe44 to be exact homozygous for apoe4 so why did apoe3 arise why did apoe2 arise we don't really know what we do know is that apoe4 appears to be protective against infections parasitic infections so is that some sort of a protective mechanism that was then less advantageous to humans as we evolved hard to say right now apoe4 is present in i would say a minority of the population with the majority of us being apoe3 i believe and a smaller fraction being apoe2 but if you look across the population broadly apoe4 is associated with a higher risk of dementia specifically alzheimer's now what we also know about our population is that the majority of us are insulin resistant and when you dig into the data as i'll show you in a moment i think that the data is less concerning for those who are insulin sensitive and there is a connection between insulin resistance and increased incidence of dementia in those with apoe4 if you're interested in our history as humans consider this paper the evolution

chunks.title: Impact of Insulin Sensitivity
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the relationship between insulin resistance and the risk of dementia associated with ApoE4. The speaker asserts that insulin-sensitive individuals may not need to be as concerned about ApoE4, citing evidence that challenges the belief that ApoE4 inevitably leads to dementia.
chunks.transcript: of human apolipa protein e isoforms gene structure protein function interaction with dietary factors you can see here from the abstract the e4 allele is associated with an increased age-related disease risk but also the ancestral form so we must ask ourselves was apoe4

chunks.title: Genetic Testing for ApoE4
chunks.summary: The speaker advises on how individuals can find out if they have the ApoE4 variant through genetic testing, including mentions of companies like 23andMe. They reflect on their own results and the implications of this genetic information for dietary choices.
chunks.transcript: not advantageous to us for millions of years of human evolution and hundreds of thousands of years of evolution as homo sapiens during which we were very likely seeking out treasuring and celebrating saturated fat from animals and animal fats it's an interesting question i think it's quite compelling to consider that and as you'll see in a moment there are multiple populations on the planet of indigenous people who live closer to a hunter-gatherer lifestyle than we do as westerners in which apoe4 doesn't look to be harmful so raises a lot of questions as for rebecca's question about how do i know you can probably get genetic testing through your physician i did 23andme this is in the news a lot today it's quite controversial apparently 23andme sold some genetic data i don't know if you guys want to do 23andme and have your genetic data available to be sold it's kind of a controversial thing but i found it interesting perhaps some russian hacker has my dna somewhere and it will come back to bite me in the future but it was powerful for me to know that i am apoe3 and other polymorphisms that i've discussed in the past i'm homozygous for mthfr 677c to t these sort of things are interesting to me and understanding how they affect my biology is important for me

chunks.title: Indigenous Populations and ApoE4
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights studies on indigenous populations, like the Chimane people, who exhibit low rates of dementia despite higher frequencies of ApoE4. The speaker argues that factors such as diet and lifestyle play a significant role in these outcomes.
chunks.transcript: as a human in general with regard to the ancestral versions of apoe4 and the risk of dementia and other issues with cognitive decline in indigenous populations consider this article the inflammatory gene variants in the chimney an indigenous bolivian population with a high infectious load chimney is spelled t-s-i-m-a-n-e and you can see from the abstract that in individuals who were carriers or had a copy or two individuals who were carriers or ap4 there was less incidence of inflammation in response to infectious load and there are other articles showing there is actually less propensity toward dementia and cognitive decline so in indigenous populations who are presumably fairly insulin sensitive without exposure to our processed diet i would point to seed

chunks.title: Cholesterol Levels and ApoE4
chunks.summary: The speaker shares findings from research in Nigerian populations that suggest ApoE4 may be protective against Alzheimer's disease under certain conditions, including lower cholesterol levels. This reinforces the complexity of the relationship between ApoE4 and cognitive decline.
chunks.transcript: oils and processed sugar specifically an apoe4 variant is protected in some populations this variant is not a clear sentence toward alzheimer's or dementia in humans there's more nuance here that we are not being told about there's also this paper looking at apoe4 in nigerian population cholesterol aoe genotype and alzheimer's in the nigerian yoruba as you can see here there was a significant interaction between cholesterol apoe4 and the risk of alzheimer's disease in the aruba a population that has lower cholesterol levels lower incidence rates of a.d compared to african populations and they found that apoe4 was protective they say here increasing levels of cholesterol and ldl were associated with an increased risk of alzheimer's disease disease in individuals without the apoe4 allele but not in those with

chunks.title: Case Study: Dietary Intervention
chunks.summary: A case study of an individual with ApoE4 demonstrates how a high-saturated fat, low-carbohydrate diet led to significant improvements in cognitive function and metabolic health. The outcomes challenge conventional dietary advice typically given to individuals with ApoE4.
chunks.transcript: apo e4 there was no significant association between levels of triglycerides and alzheimer's disease risk in those without apoe4 there's also quite an interesting case study looking at an individual with apoe4 this individual is heterozygous one copy of apoe4 here is this case study titled 8.4 the door to insulin resistant dyslipidemia and brain fog a case study this was an individual a a male who had dementia and the way that dementia is often acid is with various cognitive rating systems like the montreal cognitive assessment etc this individual was given a diet high in saturated fats and low in carbohydrates again he was heterozygous for apoe4 he was type 2 diabetic and they say that at the end of the intervention this is just one case study the patient's mocha montreal cognitive assessment improved from 23 out of 30 to 29 out of 30. uh normal is greater than 26 his type 2 diabetes reversed his pre-intervention hemoglobin a1c was 7.8 post-intervention a1c 5.5 patient achieves statistically significant improvements in uh markers of metabolic syndrome the results of the case study suggests that a clinically prescribed ketogenic diet strong potential to restore systemic insulin sensitivity metabolic sensitivity metabolic flexibility in a diabetic even an apoe4 heterozygous carrier most people within the nutritional community would point to this and say that person should not get a ketogenic diet not a high fat diet high in saturated fat because they have ab4 but everything got better in this individual his metabolic sensitivity got better his dementia improved based on his mocha rating and he lost weight so at least in this case study we can substantiate or begin to look with

chunks.title: Insulin Resistance and Alzheimer's Disease
chunks.summary: The speaker connects insulin resistance with Alzheimer's disease, citing research that indicates ApoE4's negative effects may be more pronounced in insulin-resistant individuals. They emphasize the need to reconsider blanket dietary restrictions for those with ApoE4.
chunks.transcript: more favorability toward the notion that apoe4 does not mean dementia necessarily and i would argue that is all context with the most important context being you guys will know the answer to this if you've followed any of my work in the past your insulin sensitivity consider this article demonstrated brain insulin resistance with alzheimer's disease patients is associated with igf-1 resistance irs oneness regulation and cognitive decline there is a mountain of research like this looking at what we would call type 3 diabetes aka alzheimer's we know there is brain insulin resistance associated with dementia and if you look at the research in people who are insulin resistant it looks like apoe4 heterozygosity or homozygosity having an apov4 allele could lead to a higher preponderance of problems with dementia but kind of like the ldl issue is it the apoe4 or is it the insulin resistance if you consider the bolivian aruba or the chimonae we can begin to make arguments that perhaps it is not apoe4 as much it is the insulin resistance and in those with insulin resistance does the apoe4 genotype start to look a lot worse possibly remember that the majority of our population in the west is insulin resistant and i think many researchers nutritional and physicians make this mistake we try to extend our conclusions regarding an insulin resistant population to everyone to all humans many of whom who listen to this podcast may be working toward or have achieved insulin sensitivity and i don't believe in that situation we should fear

chunks.title: Conclusion: Importance of Individual Context
chunks.summary: In closing, the speaker argues for a personalized approach to diet, advocating for the nutritional benefits of animal-based foods even for individuals with ApoE4. They caution against broad assumptions about saturated fats and encourage ongoing curiosity and skepticism around dietary guidelines.
chunks.transcript: saturated fats from animals animal foods red meat even if you have an apoe4 allele i think those foods contain nutrients that are good for your brain and very possibly protective against dementia long term so something to consider i would strongly rebel question ask for curiosity and look skeptically upon the across-the-board notion that those with aoe4 variants should not be eating
